title: Fungi & protists — the other disease-causers
examTip: AQA regularly asks you to name the type of pathogen for a given disease, describe how it spreads, and explain how it causes harm. For fungi and protists, make sure you can name the specific disease, the pathogen, its spread, and its treatment.
introTips: You already know about bacteria and viruses — this lesson adds fungi and protists to your pathogen toolkit.
Rose black spot and malaria are the two diseases AQA tests most often from this section.
Pay close attention to the mosquito as a vector for malaria — examiners love testing the distinction between the pathogen and the vector.
intro: Bacteria and viruses aren't the only things that make living things ill. Fungi and protists cause some of the most widespread diseases on the planet — from a mould that ruins rose bushes to a parasite that kills hundreds of thousands of people every year. Understanding how they work tells you how to stop them.

keyPoints.heading: Rose black spot — a fungal disease of plants
keyPoints.content: Rose black spot is a disease caused by a fungus. Black or purple spots appear on the leaves, which then turn yellow and drop off early. This is a serious problem because fewer leaves means less photosynthesis — the plant can't make enough food to grow and reproduce properly. The fungus spreads through water (rain splashing spores from leaf to leaf) and through the air (wind carries spores to new plants). Treatment: spray affected plants with fungicide, or remove and destroy the affected leaves to stop the spores spreading.
writeBullets: Symptoms: black/purple spots on leaves → leaves turn yellow → leaves drop early
Spread: water (rain splash) and wind (air-borne spores)
Treatment: fungicide spray or remove/destroy affected leaves

keyTerms.def: A fungal disease of plants causing dark spots, yellowing and early leaf drop, reducing photosynthesis
A chemical treatment that kills or inhibits the growth of fungi
A tiny reproductive unit produced by fungi that can spread through air or water to start new infections
The process by which plants use light energy to make glucose from carbon dioxide and water

keyPoints.memTip: Remember it this way: SPOT → Spots appear, Plant yellows, Only treatable with fungicide or removal, Transmitted by water and wind. The reason it matters is photosynthesis — always explain why losing leaves is bad for the plant.

keyPoints.heading: Athlete's foot — a fungal disease of humans
keyPoints.content: Fungi don't only attack plants. Athlete's foot is a common fungal infection of humans. It causes itching, soreness and flaking skin, particularly between the toes, where conditions are warm and moist — perfect for fungal growth. It spreads by direct contact with infected skin or with contaminated surfaces such as changing-room floors, shared towels, or socks. Treatment is straightforward: antifungal cream applied to the affected area kills the fungus.
writeBullets: Symptoms: itching, soreness, flaking skin — especially between toes
Spread: direct contact with infected skin, or contaminated surfaces (floors, towels)

keyTerms.def: A fungal infection of human skin, typically between the toes, causing itching and flaking
A drug or cream that kills fungi or stops them growing, used to treat fungal infections

keyPoints.memTip: Athlete's foot and rose black spot are both fungal — both are treated with something 'anti-fungal' (cream for humans, fungicide spray for plants). The word root is the same. If you can remember the parallel, you'll never confuse their treatment categories.

keyPoints.heading: Malaria — a disease caused by a protist
keyPoints.content: Malaria is caused by a protist called Plasmodium. Protists are single-celled organisms — not bacteria, not fungi, not animals. Plasmodium is a parasite: it lives and reproduces inside the body of a host, harming them in the process. It invades and destroys red blood cells, causing recurring fevers, chills, and exhaustion. Malaria can be fatal. Crucially, Plasmodium is spread not by direct contact but by the bite of a female Anopheles mosquito. The mosquito is the vector — it carries and transmits the pathogen without causing the disease itself. Prevention: mosquito nets to avoid bites, insecticides to kill mosquitoes, and antimalarial drugs taken before and during travel to high-risk areas.
writeBullets: Symptoms: recurring fever, chills, fatigue — can be fatal
Mechanism: Plasmodium invades and destroys red blood cells
Vector: female Anopheles mosquito (carries and transmits the pathogen)
Prevention: mosquito nets, insecticides, antimalarial drugs

keyTerms.def: A group of mostly single-celled organisms that are not bacteria, fungi, plants or animals — some are pathogens
The protist pathogen that causes malaria; it lives as a parasite inside red blood cells
An organism that carries and transmits a pathogen from one host to another without causing the disease itself
The genus of mosquito that acts as the vector for malaria, transmitting Plasmodium through its bite
An organism that lives in or on a host organism, gaining nutrients at the host's expense

keyPoints.memTip: The most common exam slip on malaria is saying 'the mosquito causes malaria' — it doesn't. The MOSQUITO is the VECTOR; PLASMODIUM is the PATHOGEN. Try this: 'The mosquito is the taxi, Plasmodium is the criminal.' The taxi carries the criminal to your house (your red blood cells).

keyPoints.heading: How fungi and protists cause disease — the difference
keyPoints.content: Fungi and protists both cause disease, but they do it in completely different ways. Fungi grow long threads called hyphae that push through and digest the tissues of the host — this is how rose black spot damages a leaf. The hyphae release enzymes that break down cells, causing visible damage. Protists work differently. They are single-celled parasites that enter the host's cells and live inside them. Plasmodium, for example, lives and reproduces inside red blood cells, eventually bursting them. This internal destruction is what triggers the fever and the damage of malaria.
writeBullets: Fungi: grow hyphae (thread-like structures) through host tissue; release enzymes to digest cells
Protists: single-celled; enter and live inside host cells; destroy cells from within
Fungi cause visible surface damage; protists cause internal cellular destruction

keyTerms.def: Thread-like filaments that make up the body of a fungus; they can grow through tissue and secrete digestive enzymes
An organism that lives at the expense of its host, causing harm

keyPoints.memTip: Fungi = outside-in (hyphae push through tissue from outside). Protists = inside-out (get inside your cells and wreck them from within). That contrast is exactly what an examiner wants to see.

1.question: Rose black spot causes leaves to turn yellow and drop early. Why does this harm the plant?
options: Because yellow leaves are more visible to predators
Because fewer leaves means less photosynthesis, so the plant cannot make enough food
Because dropping leaves allows the fungus to spread more easily
Because yellow leaves absorb more heat, which damages the plant
1.correctFeedback: Exactly right — leaves are the plant's photosynthesis factories. Fewer leaves means less glucose produced, so the plant is weakened.
1.wrongFeedback: Think about what leaves do. Leaves are where photosynthesis happens. If the plant loses leaves early, it loses the ability to make food — that's why the disease is so harmful.

3.question: In malaria, what is the role of the Anopheles mosquito?
options: It is the pathogen that causes malaria
It is the vector — it carries and transmits Plasmodium without causing the disease itself
It provides treatment for malaria when it bites an infected person
3.correctFeedback: Correct — the mosquito is the vector. It carries Plasmodium and delivers it into the bloodstream through its bite, but the mosquito itself doesn't cause malaria.
3.wrongFeedback: The key distinction is: Plasmodium is the PATHOGEN (it causes the disease); the Anopheles mosquito is the VECTOR (it carries and transmits the pathogen). The mosquito is not itself the cause of malaria.

commonMistakes: Saying 'the mosquito causes malaria' — the mosquito is the vector; Plasmodium (the protist) is the pathogen that causes the disease.
Confusing fungal and protist mechanisms — fungi grow hyphae through tissue; protists are single-celled and live inside host cells. These are completely different strategies.
Forgetting to explain WHY rose black spot harms the plant — it's not just 'leaves fall off'; you need to say this reduces photosynthesis and weakens the plant.
Saying antifungal cream treats rose black spot — that's for athlete's foot (humans). Plants are treated with fungicide spray, or by removing affected leaves.
Treating 'protist' as a synonym for 'bacteria' — they are completely different. Protists are larger, more complex single-celled organisms. Plasmodium is a protist, not a bacterium.
examTips: When asked about malaria, always name the pathogen (Plasmodium) AND the vector (Anopheles mosquito) separately — examiners specifically want to see you know the difference.
For rose black spot, the four-part answer pattern scores well: pathogen type (fungus) → symptoms (spots, yellow leaves, leaf drop) → spread (water/wind) → treatment (fungicide/removal).
If a question asks how a fungus causes disease, the key word is 'hyphae' — say they grow through tissue and release enzymes. If it's a protist, say it lives inside host cells.

revisionCardBullets: Rose black spot: fungal disease of plants; black spots → yellow leaves → leaf drop → less photosynthesis
Rose black spot spread: water (rain splash) and wind (spores)
Rose black spot treatment: fungicide spray OR remove and destroy affected leaves
Athlete's foot: fungal infection of humans; itching, flaking skin between toes; spread by direct contact or contaminated surfaces; treat with antifungal cream
Malaria: caused by Plasmodium (a protist); spreads via female Anopheles mosquito (vector)
Malaria symptoms: recurring fever, chills, fatigue — can be fatal; Plasmodium destroys red blood cells
Malaria prevention: mosquito nets, insecticides, antimalarial drugs
KEY distinction: mosquito = VECTOR (taxi); Plasmodium = PATHOGEN (criminal)
Fungi cause disease by growing hyphae through tissue and releasing digestive enzymes
Protists cause disease by entering and living inside host cells, destroying them from within
